petitioner_background: The petitioner is an engineer seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). At the time of filing, he was an assistant professor at West Virginia University (WVU) earning $38,523, working on robotics, expert systems, and software development. He was involved in a Department of Defense-funded project called 'Smart War Fighting Array of Reconfigurable Modules (SWARM)' for the US Navy, involving Intelligent Vision System Development for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles. His dissertation was completed at Ohio University, where his research focused on manufacturing process models and finite element methods for thermal forging.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel initially asserted that the petitioner's local and academic honors served to meet this criterion, but counsel abandoned this claim in response to the director's request for additional evidence and did not raise this criterion on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that the petitioner had not established that he meets this criterion, since the claim was abandoned and no qualifying evidence of nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards was provided.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On his CV, the petitioner claimed membership in the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME), the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), the American Design Drafting Association (ADDA), and the American Society for Engineering Education (ASEE). He submitted evidence that IEEE connects more than 360,000 members in approximately 175 countries, and that ASEE has more than 12,000 members including deans, professors, instructors, students and industry representatives. ADDA materials reflected that membership 'is available to any individual, school, institution, company or corporation' involved in design drafting. He submitted no information regarding ASME. On appeal, petitioner submitted IEEE bylaws providing that members must have either (a) a baccalaureate or higher in an IEEE-designated field, (b) a baccalaureate plus three years experience, (c) six years of experience demonstrating competence, or (d) been an executive for six years directing IEEE-field work. The petitioner did not submit bylaws of ASEE or ASME, and did not claim to be an IEEE fellow. The record also lacked membership cards or other primary evidence of membership.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the fact that IEEE is not open to the public is not decisive — the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ii) requires the association to require outstanding achievements of its members. A professional association that limits membership to professionals working in the field does not necessarily require outstanding achievements. Neither earning an undergraduate degree in engineering nor working in the field for a specific number of years is an outstanding achievement. The AAO noted that IEEE elects fellows by invitation only in recognition of unusual distinction, but the petitioner did not claim to be a fellow. The director's conclusion was not subjective but based on the plain language of the regulation. Additionally, the record lacked primary evidence of membership (membership cards), and the petitioner had not established such evidence was unavailable, so the AAO need not rely on the self-serving CV per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(2).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially submitted evidence that he served on four Master theses examining committees at WVU, where he teaches. In response to the RFE, he submitted requests to review seven manuscripts for the International Association of Science and Technology for Development (all postdating the petition filing), and reviews for an IEEE conference and ASEE after the filing date. Also after filing, a division director and professor at WVU nominated the petitioner as an ASME Manufacturing Technology Evaluator, though the record contained no evidence he was actually selected. He also submitted evidence that he chaired a Master thesis examining committee after the date of filing.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director had concluded that the petitioner met this criterion, but the AAO found this was error because the director improperly relied on evidence of reviews conducted after the date of filing. Counsel did not question the director's conclusion that serving on a thesis evaluating committee at the university where one works is an inherent duty of faculty, and the AAO concurred. Duties inherent to one's occupation cannot serve to meet this criterion. The record contained no evidence that the petitioner served as an external advisor at universities other than WVU. Under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45, 49 (Reg. Comm. 1971), eligibility must be established as of the date of filing, so post-filing manuscript and conference reviews could not be considered. The petitioner had not established he met this criterion as of the date of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted reference letters and published articles. Letters came from: (1) WVU faculty affirming he was the only faculty member with his education and experience in robotics, expert systems and software development; (2) his department head asserting he was the only qualified candidate from a national search; (3) the Professor and Dean of Engineering at WVU, summarizing his work on Intelligent Vision System Development for UAVs under the DOD SWARM project for the US Navy, development of manufacturing process models for District Event Dynamic System Optimization, thermal analysis techniques using DOE, expert system approach to improve finite element mesh generation, mixed numerical/analytical methods for heat transfer in axis-symmetric disks for US Air Force, and a new Hybrid technology for heat transfer of complex geometric objects as a CAE tool; (4) a WVU professor describing three research proposals submitted during his first two years, one funded by the Research Committee of WVU Tech and a joint proposal related to SWARM; (5) another WVU professor describing his smart vision system work for SWARM, calling him an 'outstanding young researcher' whose contributions were 'potentially' to the state and nation; (6) his dissertation advisor at Ohio University describing manufacturing process models, finite element methods for thermal forging, hybrid numerical methods, and a numerical code interfacing with commercial code like Hypermesh, calling it 'extremely critical technology for the manufacture of machine components'; and (7) a plant engineer for a North America company in Ohio who shared results with the petitioner and praised his 'ability to find critical information, analyze it and act appropriately.' The petitioner also submitted three published articles, his unpublished doctoral dissertation and Masters Thesis, and his SWARM research proposal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that while the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(v) may not expressly state that the petitioner must demonstrate a 'reaction' to his work, the regulation requires contributions be not only original but 'of major significance' — a phrase that cannot be superfluous. In a research-driven field like science, contributions of major significance would be expected to have been reproduced, confirmed, and applied by other experts. Citing Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791, 795 (Comm. 1988), and Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 (Comm. 1998), the AAO explained that letters may serve as advisory opinions but CIS makes the final determination and may give less weight to uncorroborated opinions. Letters containing mere assertions of acclaim are less persuasive than those identifying specific contributions and showing how they influenced the field. Letters from independent references previously aware of the petitioner through reputation are more persuasive than those responding to solicitations. The letters were all from the petitioner's collaborators and immediate colleagues, who could not by themselves establish national or international acclaim. Having unique expertise among faculty at a single university is not evidence of national or international standing. Determinations of worker shortages fall under DOL jurisdiction per Matter of New York State Dept. of Transp., 22 I&N Dec. 215, 221 (Comm. 1998), and are not a consideration for this classification. The Dean did not explain how contributions impacted the field, did not identify any industry adopting the models, and did not identify any demonstrably influential paper. Other letters spoke of 'potentially' making contributions rather than already having done so. The dissertation advisor did not allege the work had been implemented or cited, and the record lacked evidence of citation or letters from independent experts in the forging industry applying the work. The evidence showed a talented researcher with potential but fell short of establishing contributions of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially submitted three published articles, his unpublished doctoral dissertation, his Masters Thesis, and his SWARM research proposal. In response to the RFE, he submitted evidence of two additional published articles (both after the filing date), a third article in proceedings for a 2005 conference (also after filing), seven chapters of a book he had yet to complete or publish, and e-mail messages accepting manuscripts for future publication. On appeal, counsel asserted that the petitioner had nine articles published or presented prior to the date of filing.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded the petitioner had not established the reception of the overall engineering community. The AAO cited the Association of American Universities' Committee on Postdoctoral Education Report and Recommendations (March 31, 1998), page 5, which recognizes that postdoctoral appointees are 'expected' to publish — showing that publication alone is not automatically evidence of sustained acclaim. CIS must consider the research community's reaction to those articles. Without evidence that the petitioner had been even moderately cited or comparable evidence of the significance of his work, the AAO could not conclude his publication record was indicative of or consistent with national or international acclaim. Even if publication in and of itself were sufficient (which the AAO rejected for scientific research), the petitioner still did not meet the plain language of at least two other required criteria.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed to have played a leading or critical role for WVU, where he was employed as an assistant professor at the time of filing. Several WVU faculty asserted that the petitioner had unique education and experience among the faculty. On appeal, counsel asserted that the director's finding that the petitioner was highly regarded at WVU demonstrated employment in a critical capacity for an organization with a distinguished reputation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the petitioner's qualifications for his job and competence in fulfilling his duties are insufficient to meet this criterion. At issue are the nature of the role the petitioner was hired to fill and the reputation of the entity that hired him. The director did not question WVU's national reputation, but even assuming WVU had a distinguished reputation nationally, the AAO could not conclude that every assistant professor with unique experience and education among the faculty plays a leading or critical role for the university as a whole. The position of 'assistant professor' does not set the petitioner apart from the majority of WVU faculty, let alone nationwide.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a notice of appointment for the assistant professor position listing his salary as $38,523 for August 16, 2003 through May 14, 2004. In response to the RFE, counsel asserted the petitioner was 'recently' promoted to associate professor earning $53,672 for the academic year plus $5,200 per month over the summer, and earned $100 per hour as a consultant. Documents included: (1) Notice of Intent to Renew Appointment for 2004-2005; (2) May 12, 2005 letter promoting him to Associate Professor effective August 16, 2005 with unspecified salary; (3) May 13, 2005 letter from Columbia Mills, Inc. requesting consulting services at $100/hour; (4) May 2, 2005 letter from the Founding Director of the Center for Research on Advanced Control of Autonomous Systems and Manufacturing at WVU stating the petitioner earned $5,200/month from May 16 to July 15, 2004; (5) 2003 Form W-2 reflecting wages of $35,717.46; (6) earnings statement reflecting year-to-date earnings of $8,835.18 as of February 27, 2004; (7) DOL Employment and Training Administration statistics showing level one and four wages for engineering teachers, postsecondary in Central West Virginia at $45,070/year and $93,310/year respectively; and (8) bank statement for January 1-March 31, 2004 showing payroll deposits of $1,763.11 on March 16 and March 31, 2004. The petition was filed May 6, 2004.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: As of the date of filing, the petitioner was earning $38,523, below the level one wage for both electrical engineers ($40,893/year or $19.66/hour) and postsecondary engineering teachers ($45,070/year). Even his alleged 2005 salary increase as associate professor to $53,672 plus the possibility of an additional $10,400 during summer was well below the level four wage for postsecondary engineering teachers ($93,310/year). The record contained no evidence regarding how many hours he worked as a consultant or even whether he accepted the consulting work, so actual remuneration could not be determined. Moreover, the statutory standard is national or international acclaim, so wages must compare with the highest wages in the field nationwide. The AAO noted in a footnote that the highest ten percent of electrical engineers nationwide earn $108,070 annually and the highest ten percent of aeronautics engineers earn $113,520 annually, per U.S. Department of Labor Occupational Outlook Handbook 140 (2006-07 ed.).

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding the petitioner failed to establish that he met any of the regulatory criteria as of the date of filing. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself as an engineer to such an extent that he could be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence indicated talent as an engineer but was not persuasive that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field. The petitioner was, at the time of filing, an assistant professor earning $38,523 — a position and salary that did not set him apart from university faculty nationwide — who had not provided letters from experts outside his immediate circle of colleagues or evidence that his published articles had been influential. His eligibility claim primarily hinged on his unique training among the WVU faculty and his work on a DOD project that had produced few results as of filing, which was not indicative of national or international acclaim. The burden of proof under section 291 of the Act remained with the petitioner, who had not sustained it.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45, 49 (Reg. Comm. 1971)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791, 795 (Comm. 1988)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
Matter of New York State Dept. of Transp., 22 I&N Dec. 215, 221 (Comm. 1998)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO identified a procedural error by the director: while the director concluded the petitioner met the judging criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), the director erroneously relied on evidence of reviews conducted after the date of filing, contrary to the requirement under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak that eligibility must be established as of the date of filing. The AAO also noted that the record lacked primary evidence of memberships (membership cards) and the petitioner had not established such evidence was unavailable, so the AAO would not rely on the self-serving CV per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(2). No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
